candidates.title: Absorption as Phase Transition: Critical Behavior of Feature Hierarchy Encoding in Sparse Autoencoders
candidates.summary: Feature absorption in SAEs is a phase transition phenomenon. Using statistical physics phase transition theory, we predict sharp threshold behavior at critical sparsity values. Layer 6's absorption hotspot (0.549% rate, 9 graph components) is explained as the system being near its critical point. Key predictions: (1) sharp onset of absorption at λ_c, (2) finite-size scaling with dictionary size, (3) layer depth as temperature parameter controlling proximity to phase boundary.
hypotheses: H1: Critical sparsity threshold - absorption onset is sharp below λ_c
H2: Finite-size scaling - transition width δλ ∝ N^(-1/ν)
H3: Layer depth as temperature - Layer 6 at critical point
H4: CV difference as critical phenomenon - absorbed features have lower CV at critical point
candidates.pilot_focus: E6: Sparsity sweep on GPT-2 layer 6 to identify critical λ_c
candidates.key_evidence: E5: CV 1.07 vs 1.46 (p=0.005); E2: Layer 6 hotspot (0.549%, 9 components)
candidates.theoretical_grounding: Statistical physics phase transition theory; connects to replica symmetry breaking and ultrametric organization
candidates.novelty: First application of phase transition theory to SAE absorption with testable threshold predictions
risks: Phase transition may be gradual, not sharp
Layer 6 hotspot may not replicate in other models
CV difference may be frequency artifact
mitigations: Fallback to rate-distortion framework if onset is gradual
Match on activation frequency in CV analysis

candidates.title: Absorption-Adjusted Circuit Discovery: Quantifying and Correcting Feature Absorption Bias
candidates.summary: Feature absorption systematically biases circuit discovery toward child-feature circuits. When ablating absorbed features, output changes are spuriously attributed to child circuits. This causes parent circuits to be rediscovered at deeper layers. We propose bias-corrected circuit analysis that accounts for absorption.
hypotheses: Absorption-adjusted circuit analysis identifies 30% more parent-feature circuits
candidates.pilot_focus: Validate absorption bias in circuit discovery on 20 pairs from layer 6
candidates.key_evidence: E5: CV difference suggests absorbed features are 'stable'; E4: negative co-occurrence correlation
candidates.theoretical_grounding: Causal inference framework; circuit analysis methodology
candidates.novelty: First study connecting absorption to circuit misattribution in interpretability
risks: Basu et al. negative result may make circuit analysis moot
mitigations: Focus on understanding WHY absorbed features resist intervention

candidates.title: Layer-Dependent Feature Absorption: A Projection-Based Quantification
candidates.summary: Absorption rates follow a non-monotonic pattern across network depth (peak in mid-layers 5-9), robust across architectures when using probe projection metrics. This addresses the gap in systematic cross-layer quantification using the more robust SAEBench projection metric.
hypotheses: Non-monotonic absorption pattern across layers with mid-layer peak
Projection metric shows different layer profile than ablation metric
candidates.pilot_focus: Run projection-based absorption on GPT-2 layer 6 (10 min pilot)
candidates.key_evidence: E2: Layer 6 hotspot across multiple metrics; SAEBench robustness
candidates.theoretical_grounding: SAEBench evaluation methodology; probe projection approach
candidates.novelty: First systematic cross-layer quantification using projection metrics
risks: Projection metric may be unreliable on GPT-2

key_pilot_evidence.E2: Layer 6 hotspot: 0.549% rate, 9 components, 0.559 mean edge weight
key_pilot_evidence.E5: CV 1.07 vs 1.46 (p=0.005) for absorbed vs non-absorbed
